title: Probing pairing symmetries through quasiparticle interference in chiral Bloch bands
authors: Sayan Banerjee, Subrata Mandal, Peter P. Orth, Mathias S. Scheurer
abstract: Recent experiments in van der Waals multi-layer systems have demonstrated that superconductivity can emerge from symmetry-reduced, chiral normal states. We here provide a theory for quasiparticle interference (QPI) of superconductors with chiral Bloch bands. Our analysis reveals how the non-trivial quantum geometry of the Bloch states crucially affects the interference pattern even in the normal state, inducing significant sublattice dependence. In the superconducting state, the behavior becomes more complex due to the interplay of the quantum geometry of the Bogoliubov quasiparticles with the momentum-dependent phase of the order parameter. We reveal how the spatial dependence of the local spectral function around impurities can be used to distinguish between different candidate pairing states, both with zero and finite center-of-mass momentum. Our work thus provides guidance to interpreting QPI patterns in materials with chiral bands, which may be useful when probing the rich physics of pairing in such systems.
summary: This theoretical work provides a comprehensive method to interpret quasiparticle interference (QPI) patterns in superconductors with chiral Bloch bands. By analyzing the impurity-induced spectral function, the authors show that the resulting QPI pattern carries a unique fingerprint related to the pairing symmetry, enabling the distinction between chiral and achiral superconducting orders. This offers a powerful diagnostic tool for experimental condensed matter physics.
main_problem: The paper develops a theoretical framework to use quasiparticle interference (QPI) measurements to distinguish between different candidate pairing symmetries in superconductors exhibiting chiral Bloch bands.
main_result: The QPI pattern, analyzed via the superconducting spectral function, can reveal the intrinsic phase winding number associated with the pairing symmetry, allowing differentiation between chiral and achiral pairing states.
method: The analysis involves calculating the impurity-induced change in the superconducting spectral function ($\delta A_{SC}$) using the Born approximation within the Nambu formalism, followed by Fourier transformation to momentum space.
model_or_system: The study models superconductivity in van der Waals multi-layer systems (like graphene) possessing chiral Bloch bands. The theoretical framework utilizes a minimal two-band model and extends the analysis from the normal state to the superconducting state via the Bogoliubov-de Gennes formalism.
key_observables: The primary observable is the spatially resolved QPI modulation ($\delta A_{SC}^{\alpha eta}(\mathbf{r})$) and its corresponding momentum-space signature ($\delta G_{SC}^{\alpha eta}(\omega, \delta \mathbf{k})$).
important_parameters:
The analysis distinguishes between zero-momentum ($q=0$) and finite-momentum ($q
eq 0$) pairing, and examines the contributions from normal and anomalous terms.
main_assumptions: The calculations rely on the Born approximation for impurity scattering and project the full Green's function onto the active low-energy band.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the QPI patterns for various pairing symmetries (achiral vs. chiral) and momentum transfers, showing how the internal intensity variations reflect the quantum geometry and pairing structure.
paper_structure:
The paper first establishes the normal-state QPI theory, then extends the formalism to the superconducting state using the Nambu basis. It systematically analyzes the QPI signatures for $q=0$ and $q
eq 0$ pairing, comparing the results for different pairing symmetries.
why_interesting: The detailed theoretical mapping between measurable electronic signatures (QPI patterns) and fundamental microscopic parameters (pairing symmetry, quantum geometry) provides a powerful diagnostic tool applicable to complex correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Normal state results. (i) Band structure of the low-energy Hamiltonian in Eq. (1) along the cut k = (kx, 0), showing the upper (E+ k ) and lower (E− k ) branches. One- dimensional cuts of the leading-order impurity-induced cor- rection in the spectral function, δANS αβ(ω) as a function of po- sition x for (ii) top-top (tt), (iii) top-bottom (tb), and (iv) bottom-bottom (bb) configurations. The bottom-top combi- nation is identical to the top-bottom combination. We ex- press momenta and lengths in units of k∗= (u0/w0)1/4 and 1/k∗, respectively, and energies in units of u0. Here we set u0 = w0 = 1, µ = 0, ω = −2, V0 = 1 and η = 0.01.

FIG. 2. Zero momentum superconductivity. Impurity- induced change in the superconducting spectral function, δASC αβ(r, ω) [Eq. (18)] for q = 0 plotted in real space for achi- ral (first row) and chiral (second row) pairing states in the (i, iii) top-top and (ii, iv) bottom-bottom configurations for ω = 1.05, µ = 2. Insets show the corresponding momentum- space QPI patterns obtained by Fourier transform. All other parameters are the same as Fig. 1.

FIG. 3. Finite momentum superconductivity. Impurity-induced change in δASC αβ(r, ω) in real space and their corresponding QPI patterns (insets) for achiral SC in (i) top-top and (ii) bottom-bottom combination; chiral SC in (iii) top-top and (iv) bottom-bottom combination at q ̸= 0. Panel (v) shows the momentum-space density map of the allowed scattering processes, determined by the Fermi surface geometry (inset), which sets the accessible scattering wavevectors visible in the QPI patterns of panels (i)–(iv).

FIG. 4. Separating normal and anomalous contribu- tions. Normal and anomalous contributions to δA(r, ω), de- noted as I and II, respectively, and corresponding to the first and second term in Eq. (17). Panel (i) depicts the contribu- tions for the tt configuration corresponding to both achiral and chiral types of pairing. Panel (ii) depicts the same, but for the bb configuration. The corresponding QPI patterns are shown in the insets. While both contributions are of the same order in the tt case (allowing for a distinction between the two pairings), the anomalous one is much smaller than the normal one in the bb configuration.

FIG. 5. Normal state with trigonal warping. This figure shows the leading-order change in the spectral function δANS αβ(ω) as a function of position, with corresponding QPI maps in momentum space shown in the insets for (i) top–top (tt), (ii) top– bottom (tb), (iii) bottom–top and (iv) bottom–bottom (bb) configurations. Here we have used u0 = w0 = 1, µ = 0, V0 = 1, v0 = 0.7, w1 = 0.3, w2 = 0.2, w3 = 0.1, ω = −2 and η = 0.05.
